00412|  Prerequisites
00413| Semiconductor
00414| пӮ·
00415| пӮ·
00416| пӮ·
00417| пӮ·
00418| 
00419| пӮ·
00420| пӮ·
00421| пӮ·
00422| пӮ·
00423| 
00424| Materials that permit flow of electrons are called conductors (e.g., gold, silver, copper,
00425| etc.).
00426| Materials that block flow of electrons are called insulators (e.g., rubber, glass, Teflon, mica,
00427| etc.).
00428| Materials whose conductivity falls between those of conductors and insulators are called
00429| semiconductors.
00430| Semiconductors are вҖңpart-timeвҖқ conductors whose conductivity can be controlled.
00431| 
00432| Silicon
00433| Silicon is the most common material used to build semiconductor devices.
00434| Si is the main ingredient of sand and it is estimated that a cubic mile of seawater contains
00435| 15,000 tons of Si.
00436| Si is spun and grown into a crystalline structure and cut into wafers to make electronic
00437| devices.
00438| Atoms in a pure silicon wafer contains four electrons in outer orbit (called valence
00439| electrons).
00440| вҖ“ Germanium is another semiconductor material with four valence electrons.
00441| 
00442|  пӮ·
00443| 
00444| пӮ·
00445| 
00446| In the crystalline lattice structure of Si, the valence electrons of every Si atom are locked
00447| up in covalent bonds with the valence electrons of four neighboring Si atoms.
00448| вҖ“ In pure form, Si wafer does not contain any free charge carriers.
00449| вҖ“ An applied voltage across pure Si wafer does not yield electron flow through the wafer.
00450| вҖ“ A pure Si wafer is said to act as an insulator.
00451| In order to make useful semiconductor devices, materials such as phosphorus (P) and boron
00452| (B) are added to Si to change SiвҖҷs conductivity.
00453| 
00454| N-Type Silicon
00455| пӮ·
00456| пӮ·
00457| 
00458| пӮ·
00459| 
00460| пӮ·
00461| 
00462| Pentavalent impurities such as phosphorus, arsenic, antimony, and bismuth have 5 valence
00463| electrons.
00464| When phosphorus impurity is added to Si, every phosphorus atomвҖҷs four valence electrons
00465| are locked up in covalent bond with valence electrons of four neighboring Si atoms.
00466| However, the 5th valence electron of phosphorus atom does not find a binding electron and
00467| thus remains free to float. When a voltage is applied across the silicon-phosphorus mixture,
00468| free electrons migrate toward the positive voltage end.
00469| When phosphorus is added to Si to yield the above effect, we say that Si is doped with
00470| phosphorus. The resulting mixture is called N-type silicon (N: negative charge carrier
00471| silicon).
00472| The pentavalent impurities are referred to as donor impurities.
00473| 
00474| P-Type Silicon
00475| пӮ·
00476| 
00477| Trivalent impurities e.g., boron, aluminum, indium, and gallium have 3 valence
00478| electrons.
00479| 
00480|  пӮ·
00481| 
00482| пӮ·
00483| пӮ·
00484| 
00485| When boron is added to Si, every boron atomвҖҷs three valence electrons are locked up
00486| in covalent bond with valence electrons of three neighboring Si atoms. However, a
00487| vacant spot вҖңholeвҖқ is created within the covalent bond between one boron atom and a
00488| neighboring Si atom. The holes are considered to be positive charge carriers. When a
00489| voltage is applied across the silicon-boron mixture, a hole moves toward the negative
00490| voltage end while a neighboring electron fills in its place.
00491| When boron is added to Si to yield the above effect, we say that Si is doped with boron.
00492| The resulting mixture is called P-type silicon (P: positive charge carrier silicon).
00493| The trivalent impurities are referred to as acceptor impurities.
00494| 
00495| Module 2
00496| Diode A diode is a semiconductor device, made from a small piece of semiconductor
00497| material, such as silicon, in which half is doped as a p region and half is doped as an n
00498| region with a pn junction and depletion region in between.
00499| Depletion region: immobile positive charge at n-side and immobile negative charge at pside near the junction acts like a barrier or wall and prevent the further flow of free electrons
00500| and holes. The region near the junction where flow of charges carriers are decreased over
00501| a given time and finally results in empty charge carriers or full of immobile charge carriers
00502| is called depletion region.
00503| The depletion region is also called as depletion zone, depletion layer, space charge region,
00504| or space charge layer. The depletion region acts like a wall between p-type and n-type
00505| semiconductor and prevents further flow of free electrons and holes.
00506| The p region is called the anode and n region is called the cathode. It conducts current in
00507| one direction and offers high resistance in other direction. The basic diode structure and
00508| symbol are shown in Fig.1.
00509| 
00510| Figure 1
00511| 
00512|  Forward Bias Bias is the application of a dc voltage to a diode to make it either conduct or
00513| block current. Forward bias is the condition that allows current through the pn junction.
00514| This external bias voltage is designated as VBIAS. The resistor limits the forward current
00515| to a value that will not damage the diode. In the forward bias, the negative side of VBIAS
00516| is connected to the n region of the diode and the positive side is connected to the p region.
00517| The bias voltage VBIAS, must be greater than the barrier potential; bias must be greater
00518| than 0.3V for germanium or 0.7V for silicon diodes.
00519| 
00520| Figure 2: A diode connected for forward bias.
00521| Negative side of bias voltage вҖҳpushesвҖҷ free electrons towards pn junction. The negative
00522| side of the source also provides a continuous flow of electrons through the external
00523| connection (conductor) and into the n region as shown in Figure 3. The bias-voltage source
00524| imparts sufficient energy to the free electrons for them to overcome the barrier potential of
00525| the depletion region and move on through into the p region. Since unlike charges attract,
00526| the positive side of the bias-voltage source attracts the valence electrons toward the left
00527| end of the p region. The holes in the p region provide the medium for these valence
00528| electrons to move through the p region. The holes, (majority in p region), move to the right
00529| toward the junction. As the electrons flow out of the p region through the external
00530| connection (conductor), these electrons become conduction electrons in the metal
00531| conductor. As more electrons move into the depletion region, the number of positive ions
00532| is reduced. As more holes flow into the depletion region on the other side of the pn junction,
00533| the number of negative ions is reduced. This reduction in positive and negative ions causes
00534| the depletion region to narrow.
00535| 
00536| Figure 3: A forward-biased diode showing the flow of majority carriers and the voltage
00537| due to the barrier potential across the depletion region.
00538| Reverse Bias Reverse bias is the condition that essentially prevents current through the
00539| diode. Figure 4 shows a dc voltage source connected across a diode in the direction to
00540| produce reverse bias. The positive side of VBIAS is connected to the n region of the diode
00541| and the negative side is connected to the p region. Also, note that the depletion region is
00542| shown much wider than in forward bias or equilibrium. The positive side of the biasvoltage source pulls the free electrons, (majority in n region), away from the pn junction.
00543| 
00544|  As electrons move away from junction, more positive ions are created. This results in a
00545| widening of the depletion region and a depletion of majority carriers.
00546| 
00547| Figure 4
00548| In p region, electrons from negative side of battery enter as valence electrons. It moves
00549| from hole to hole toward the depletion region, creating more negative ions. This can be
00550| viewed as holes being pulled towards the negative side. The electric field increases in
00551| strength until the potential across depletion region equals the bias voltage. At this point,
00552| very small reverse current exist that can usually be neglected.
00553| Reverse Breakdown
00554| Normally, the reverse current is so small that it can be neglected. If the external reversebias
00555| voltage is increased to a value called the breakdown voltage, the reverse current will
00556| drastically increase. The high reverse-bias voltage imparts energy to the free minority
00557| electrons so that as they speed through the p region, they collide with atoms with enough
00558| energy to knock valence electrons out of orbit and into the conduction band. The newly
00559| created conduction electrons have high energy, and repeat the process, they quickly
00560| multiply. They have high energy to move though pn junction, and not combine with holes.
00561| The multiplication of conduction electrons just discussed is known as the avalanche effect.
00562| V/I Characteristic
00563| Fig.5 shows the static voltage current characteristics for a low power P-N junction diode.
00564| 
00565| Figure 5
00566| Forward characteristic: When the diode is forward biased and applied voltage is increased
00567| from zero hardly any current flows through the device in the beginning. It is so because the
00568| external voltage is being opposed by the internal barrier voltage VB whose value is 0.7 V
00569| for Si and 0.3 V for Ge. As soon as VB is neutralized, current through the diode increases
00570| 
00571|  rapidly with increasing applied battery voltage. It is found that as little a voltage as 1.0 V
00572| produces a forward current of about 50 mA.
00573| Reverse characteristic: When the diode is reverse biased majority carriers are blocked and
00574| only a small current (due to minority carriers) flows through the diode. As the reverse
00575| voltage is increased from zero, the reverse current very quickly reaches its maximum or
00576| saturation value I0 which is also known as leakage current. It is of order of nano ampers
00577| (nA) for Si and micro ampers (ВөA) for Ge. As seen from fig.5 when reverse voltage VBR,
00578| the leakage current 8 suddenly and sharply increases, the curve indicating zero resistance
00579| at this point.
00580| Transistor: Introduction
00581| пӮ·
00582| пӮ·
00583| пӮ·
00584| 
00585| пӮ·
00586| пӮ·
00587| пӮ·
00588| 
00589| Beside diodes, the most popular semiconductor devices is transistors.
00590| Transistors are often said to be the most significant invention of the 20th Century.
00591| If cells are the building blocks of life, transistors are the building blocks of the
00592| digital revolution. Without transistors, the technological wonders you use every day
00593| -- cell phones, computers, cars -- would be vastly different, if they existed at all.
00594| Transistors are more complex and can be used in many ways.
00595| Most important feature: can amplify signals and as switch.
00596| Amplification can make weak signal strong (make sounds louder and signal levels
00597| greater), in general, provide function called Gain.
00598| 
00599| Transistor Definition
00600| Transistor is an electronic device made of three layers of semiconductor material that can act as
00601| an insulator and a conductor. The three layered transistor is also known as the bipolar junction
00602| transistor.
00603| Transistor Structure
00604| A transistor has three doped regions.
00605| пӮ· For both types, the base is a narrow region sandwiched between the larger collector and
00606| emitter regions.
00607| пӮ· The emitter region is heavily doped and its job is to emit carriers into the base.
00608| пӮ· The base region is very thin and lightly doped.
00609| пӮ· Most of the current carriers injected into the base pass on to the collector.
00610| пӮ· The collector region is moderately doped and is the largest of all three regions.
00611| пӮ· The junction between emitter and base is called emitter-base junction while the junction
00612| between the collector and base is called collector-base junction.
00613| пӮ· As the base is thin and tightly doped, and the emitter is heavily doped, it is wider when
00614| compared to base, the width of the collector is more when compared to both base and
00615| emitter.
00616| 
00617|  пӮ·
00618| 
00619| пӮ·
00620| пӮ·
00621| пӮ·
00622| пӮ·
00623| пӮ·
00624| пӮ·
00625| 
00626| In order to distinguish the emitter and collector an arrow is included in the emitter. The
00627| direction of the arrow depends on the conventional flow of current when emitter base
00628| junction is forward biased.
00629| 
00630| Transistor Construction
00631| BJT is bipolar because both holes (+) and electrons (-) will take part in the current flow
00632| through the device
00633| N-type regions contains free electrons (negative carriers)
00634| P-type regions contains free holes (positive carriers)
00635| 2 types of BJT вҖ“ NPN transistor вҖ“ PNP transistor
00636| The transistor regions are: вҖ“
00637| Emitter (E) вҖ“ send the carriers into the base region and then on to the collector. The section
00638| of one side that supplies carriers is called emitter. Emitter is always forward biased wr to
00639| 
00640|  пӮ·
00641| пӮ·
00642| 
00643| base so it can supply carrier. For вҖңnpn transistorвҖқ emitter supply holes to its junction. For
00644| вҖңpnp transistorвҖқ emitter supply electrons to its junction.
00645| Base (B) вҖ“ acts as control region. It can allow none, some or many carriers to flow. The
00646| middle section which forms two pn junction between emitter and collector is called Base.
00647| Collector (C) вҖ“ collects the carriers. The section on the other side that collects carrier is
00648| called collector. The collector is always reversed biased wrt to base. For вҖңnpn transistorвҖқ
00649| collector receives holes to its junction. For вҖңpnp transistorвҖқ collector receives electrons to
00650| its junction.
00651| 
00652| Figure: NPN Transistor Structure
00653| Operating regions of a transistor
00654| Unbiased Transistor
00655| пӮ·
00656| пӮ·
00657| пӮ·
00658| 
00659| пӮ·
00660| пӮ·
00661| пӮ·
00662| 
00663| When the three terminal of the transistor kept open it is called unbiased transistor.
00664| Since transistor is like two p-n junction diodes connected back to back, there are
00665| depletion region at both the junctions- Emitter junction and Collector junction.
00666| During diffusion process, depletion region penetrates more deeply into the lightly
00667| doped side in order to include an equal number of impurity atoms in the each side of
00668| the junction.
00669| As shown in the figure, depletion region at emitter junction penetrates less in the
00670| heavily doped emitter and extends more in the base region.
00671| Similarly, depletion region at collector junction penetrates less in the heavily doped
00672| collector and extends more in to base region.
00673| As collector is slightly less doped than the emitter, the depletion layer width at the
00674| collector junction is more than the depletion layer width at the emitter junction.
00675| 
00676|  Figure: Unbiased NPN transistor
00677| 
00678| Figure: Unbiased PNP transistor
00679| Transistor biasing
00680| In electronics, 'biasing' usually refers to a fixed DC voltage or current applied to a terminal
00681| of an electronic component such as a diode, transistor or vacuum tube in a circuit in which
00682| AC signals are also present, in order to establish proper operating conditions for the
00683| 
00684|  component. Biasing is the process of providing DC voltage which helps in the functioning
00685| of the circuit.
00686| A transistor can be operated in three different regions as
00687| пӮ· Active region
00688| пӮ· Saturation region
00689| пӮ· Cut-off region
00690| Active region
00691| 
00692| пӮ·
00693| пӮ·
00694| 
00695| The transistor is said to be operated in active region when the emitter-base junction is
00696| forward biased and collectorвҖ“base junction is reverse biased.
00697| The collector current is said to have two current components one is due to the forward
00698| biasing of EB junction and the other is due to reverse biasing of CB junction.
00699| 
00700| Saturation region
00701| 
00702| пӮ·
00703| пӮ·
00704| 
00705| Transistor is said to be operated in saturation region when both EB junction and CB
00706| junction are forward biased as shown in Fig.
00707| When transistor is operated in saturation region Ic increases rapidly for a very small change
00708| in Vc.
00709| 
00710| Cut-off region
00711| 
00712|  пӮ·
00713| пӮ·
00714| 
00715| When both EB junction and CB junction are reverse biased, the transistor is said to be
00716| operated in cutoff region.
00717| The current in the transistor is very small and thus when a transistor in this region it is
00718| assumed to be in off state.
00719| Transistor Working
00720| Working of NPN transistor
00721| 
00722| When external voltage is applied to an npn transistor, it is said to be a biased npn transistor.
00723| Depending on the polarity of the applied voltage, the npn transistor can be operated in three modes:
00724| active mode, cutoff mode and saturation mode.
00725| 
00726| The npn transistor is often operated in active mode because in active mode the npn transistor
00727| amplifies the electric current.
00728| So letвҖҷs see how an npn transistor works in active mode.
00729| Let us consider an npn transistor as shown in the below figure. In the below figure, the emitterbase junction is forward biased by the DC voltage VEE and base-collector junction is reverse biased
00730| by the DC voltage VCC.
00731| 
00732|  Emitter-base junction:
00733| Due to the forward bias, a large number of free electrons in the left side n-region (emitter)
00734| experience a repulsive force from the negative terminal of the DC battery and also they experience
00735| an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. When the DC voltage exceeds the knee
00736| voltage, the free electrons start flowing from emitter to base. In the similar way, holes in base
00737| experience a repulsive force from the positive terminal of the battery and also experience an
00738| attractive force from the negative terminal of the battery. As a result, the holes start flowing from
00739| base to emitter.
00740| 
00741|  Thus, the depletion width at the emitter-base junction reduces by applying the forward bias
00742| voltage.
00743| We know that electric current means flow of charge carriers. The free electrons (negative charge
00744| carriers) flow from emitter to base whereas holes (positive charge carriers) flow from base to
00745| emitter. These charge carriers conduct electric current. However, the conventional
00746| current direction is same as the direction of holes.
00747| Thus, the electric current flows from base to emitter.
00748| Base-collector junction:
00749| 
00750| Due to the reverse bias, a large number of free electrons in the right side n-region (collector)
00751| experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. Hence, the free electrons
00752| move away from the junction and flow towards the positive terminal of the battery. As a result, a
00753| large number of neutral collector atoms loses electrons and becomes positive ions. On the other
00754| hand, holes in the p-region (base) experience an attractive force from the negative terminal of the
00755| battery. Hence, the holes move away from the junction and flow towards the negative terminal of
00756| the battery. As a result, a large number of neutral base atoms gains electrons and becomes negative
00757| ions.
00758| Thus, the width of depletion region increases at base-collector junction. In other words, the number
00759| of positive and negative ions increases at the base-collector junction.
00760| 
00761|  Collector-base-emitter current:
00762| 
00763| The free electrons that are flowing from emitter to base due to forward bias will combine with the
00764| holes in the base. However, the base is very thin and lightly doped. So only, a small percentage of
00765| emitter free electrons combines with the holes in the base region. The remaining large number of
00766| free electrons will cross the base region and reaches to the collector region. This is due to the
00767| positive supply voltage applied at collector. Hence, free electrons flow from emitter to collector.
00768| At collector, both the emitter free electrons and collector free electrons produces current by
00769| flowing towards the positive terminal of the battery, so IE = IB + IC. Therefore, an amplified current
00770| is produced at the output.
00771| In npn transistor, the electric current is majorly conducted by free electrons, however the current
00772| conduction in BJT is due to both holes and electrons.
00773| Working of a Biased pnp transistor
00774| When external voltage is applied to a pnp transistor, it is said to be a biased pnp transistor.
00775| Depending on the polarity of the applied voltage, the pnp transistor can be operated in three modes:
00776| active mode, cutoff mode and saturation mode.
00777| 
00778|  The pnp transistor is often operated in active mode because in active mode the pnp transistor
00779| amplifies the electric current.
00780| So letвҖҷs see how a pnp transistor works in active mode.
00781| Let us consider a pnp transistor as shown in the below figure. In the below figure, the emitter-base
00782| junction is forward biased by the DC voltage VEE and base-collector junction is reverse biased by
00783| the DC voltage VCC.
00784| Emitter-base junction:
00785| 
00786| Due to the forward bias, a large number of holes in the left side p-region (emitter) experience a
00787| repulsive force from the positive terminal of the DC battery and also they experience an attractive
00788| force from the negative terminal of the battery. When the DC voltage exceeds the knee voltage,
00789| the holes start flowing from emitter to base. In the similar way, free electrons in base experience a
00790| 
00791|  repulsive force from the negative terminal of the battery and also experience an attractive force
00792| from the positive terminal of the battery. As a result, the free electrons start flowing from base to
00793| emitter.
00794| The majority carriers holes carry most of the current from emitter to base. Thus, the electric current
00795| flows from emitter to base.
00796| This electric current flow reduces the width of the depletion region at emitter-base junction.
00797| Base-collector junction:
00798| 
00799| Due to the reverse bias, a large number of holes in the right side n-region (collector) experience
00800| an attractive force from the negative terminal of the battery. Hence, the holes move away from the
00801| junction and flow towards the negative terminal of the battery. As a result, a large number of
00802| neutral collector atoms gains electrons and becomes negative ions. On the other hand, free
00803| electrons in the n-region (base) experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the
00804| battery. Hence, the free electrons move away from the junction and flow towards the positive
00805| terminal of the battery. As a result, a large number of neutral base atoms loses electrons and
00806| becomes positive ions.
00807| Thus, the width of depletion region increases at base-collector junction. In other words, the number
00808| of positive and negative ions increases at the base-collector junction.
00809| 
00810|  Emitter-base-collector current:
00811| 
00812| The holes that are flowing from emitter to base due to forward bias will combines with the free
00813| electrons in the base. However, the base is very thin and lightly doped. So only, a small percentage
00814| of emitter holes will combine with the free electrons in the base region. The remaining large
00815| number of holes will cross the base region and reaches to the collector region. This is due to the
00816| negative supply voltage applied at collector. Hence, the holes flow from emitter to collector. At
00817| collector, both the emitter holes and collector holes produces current by flowing towards the
00818| negative terminal of the battery so, IE = IB + IC. Therefore, an amplified current is produced at the
00819| output.
00820| In pnp transistor, the electric current is majorly conducted by holes, however the current
00821| conduction in BJT is due to both holes and electrons.

00919| Transistor Configurations
00920| Common Base (CB) configuration
00921| In common base configuration, emitter is the input terminal, collector is the output terminal and
00922| base terminal is connected as a common terminal for both input and output. That means the emitter
00923| terminal and common base terminal are known as input terminals whereas the collector terminal
00924| and common base terminal are known as output terminals.
00925| In common base configuration, the base terminal is grounded so the common base configuration
00926| is also known as grounded base configuration. Sometimes common base configuration is referred
00927| to as common base amplifier, CB amplifier, or CB configuration.
00928| 
00929|  The input signal is applied between the emitter and base terminals while the corresponding output
00930| signal is taken across the collector and base terminals. Thus the base terminal of a transistor is
00931| common for both input and output terminals and hence it is named as common base configuration.
00932| The supply voltage between base and emitter is denoted by VBE while the supply voltage between
00933| collector and base is denoted by VCB.
00934| As mentioned earlier, in every configuration, the base-emitter junction JE is always forward biased
00935| and collector-base junction JC is always reverse biased. Therefore, in common base configuration,
00936| the base-emitter junction JE is forward biased and collector-base junction JC is reverse biased.
00937| The common base configuration for both NPN and PNP transistors is shown in the below figure.
00938| 
00939|  From the above circuit diagrams of npn and pnp transistors, it can be seen that for both npn and
00940| pnp transistors, the input is applied to the emitter and the output is taken from the collector. The
00941| common terminal for both the circuits is the base.
00942| Current flow in common base amplifier
00943| For the sake of understanding, let us consider NPN transistor in common base configuration.
00944| The npn transistor is formed when a single p-type semiconductor layer is sandwiched between
00945| two n-type semiconductor layers.
00946| 
00947|  The base-emitter junction JE is forward biased by the supply voltage VBE while the collector-base
00948| junction JC is reverse biased by the supply voltage VCB.
00949| Due to the forward bias voltage VBE, the free electrons (majority carriers) in the emitter region
00950| experience a repulsive force from the negative terminal of the battery similarly holes (majority
00951| carriers) in the base region experience a repulsive force from the positive terminal of the battery.
00952| As a result, free electrons start flowing from emitter to base similarly holes start flowing from base
00953| to emitter. Thus free electrons which are flowing from emitter to base and holes which are flowing
00954| from base to emitter conducts electric current. The actual current is carried by free electrons which
00955| are flowing from emitter to base. However, we follow the conventional current direction which is
00956| from base to emitter. Thus electric current is produced at the base and emitter region.
00957| 
00958|  The free electrons which are flowing from emitter to base will combine with the holes in the base
00959| region similarly the holes which are flowing from base to emitter will combine with the electrons
00960| in the emitter region.
00961| From the above figure, it is seen that the width of the base region is very thin. Therefore, only a
00962| small percentage of free electrons from emitter region will combine with the holes in the base
00963| region and the remaining large number of free electrons cross the base region and enters into the
00964| collector region. A large number of free electrons which entered into the collector region will
00965| experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. Therefore, the free electrons
00966| in the collector region will flow towards the positive terminal of the battery. Thus, electric current
00967| is produced in the collector region.
00968| The electric current produced at the collector region is primarily due to the free electrons from the
00969| emitter region similarly the electric current produced at the base region is also primarily due to the
00970| free electrons from emitter region. Therefore, the emitter current is greater than the base current
00971| and collector current. The emitter current is the sum of base current and collector current.
00972| IE = IB + IC
00973| We know that emitter current is the input current and collector current is the output current.
00974| The output collector current is less than the input emitter current, so the current gain of this
00975| amplifier is actually less than 1. In other words, the common base amplifier attenuates the electric
00976| current rather than amplifying it.
00977| 
00978|  The base-emitter junction JE at input side acts as a forward biased diode. So the common base
00979| amplifier has a low input impedance (low opposition to incoming current). On the other hand, the
00980| collector-base junction JC at output side acts somewhat like a reverse biased diode. So the common
00981| base amplifier has high output impedance.
00982| Therefore, the common base amplifier provides a low input impedance and high output impedance.
00983| Transistors with low input impedance and high output impedance provide a high voltage gain.
00984| Even though the voltage gain is high, the current gain is very low and the overall power gain of
00985| the common base amplifier is low as compared to the other transistor amplifier configurations.
00986| The common base transistor amplifiers are primarily used in the applications where low input
00987| impedance is required.
00988| The common base amplifier is mainly used as a voltage amplifier or current buffer.
00989| This type of transistor arrangement is not very common and is not as widely used as the other two
00990| transistor configurations.
00991| The working principle of pnp transistor with CB configuration is same as the npn transistor with
00992| CB configuration. The only difference is in npn transistor free electrons conduct most of the current
00993| whereas in pnp transistor the holes conduct most of the current.
00994| To fully describe the behavior of a transistor with CB configuration, we need two set of
00995| characteristics: they are
00996| пӮ·
00997| 
00998| Input characteristics
00999| 
01000| пӮ·
01001| 
01002| Output characteristics.
01003| 
01004| Input characteristics
01005| The input characteristics describe the relationship between input current (IE) and the input voltage
01006| (VBE).
01007| First, draw a vertical line and horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal line
01008| represents x-axis. The input current or emitter current (IE) is taken along the y-axis (vertical line)
01009| and the input voltage (VBE) is taken along the x-axis (horizontal line).
01010| To determine the input characteristics, the output voltage VCB (collector-base voltage) is kept
01011| constant at zero volts and the input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage
01012| levels. For each voltage level of the input voltage (VBE), the input current (IE) is recorded on a
01013| paper or in any other form.
01014| A curve is then drawn between input current IE and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
01015| VCB (0 volts).
01016| 
01017|  Next, the output voltage (VCB) is increased from zero volts to a certain voltage level (8 volts) and
01018| kept constant at 8 volts. While increasing the output voltage (VCB), the input voltage (VBE) is kept
01019| constant at zero volts. After we kept the output voltage (VCB) constant at 8 volts, the input voltage
01020| VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of the input
01021| voltage (VBE), the input current (IE) is recorded on a paper or in any other form.
01022| A curve is then drawn between input current IE and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
01023| VCB (8 volts).
01024| This is repeated for higher fixed values of the output voltage (VCB).
01025| When output voltage (VCB) is at zero volts and emitter-base junction JE is forward biased by the
01026| input voltage (VBE), the emitter-base junction acts like a normal p-n junction diode. So the input
01027| characteristics are same as the forward characteristics of a normal pn junction diode.
01028| The cut in voltage of a silicon transistor is 0.7 volts and germanium transistor is 0.3 volts. In our
01029| case, it is a silicon transistor. So from the above graph, we can see that after 0.7 volts, a small
01030| increase in input voltage (VBE) will rapidly increase the input current (IE).
01031| When the output voltage (VCB) is increased from zero volts to a certain voltage level (8 volts), the
01032| emitter current flow will be increased which in turn reduces the depletion region width at emitterbase junction. As a result, the cut in voltage will be reduced. Therefore, the curves shifted towards
01033| the left side for higher values of output voltage VCB.
01034| 
01035|  Output characteristics
01036| The output characteristics describe the relationship between output current (IC) and the output
01037| voltage (VCB).
01038| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
01039| line represents x-axis. The output current or collector current (IC) is taken along the y-axis (vertical
01040| line) and the output voltage (VCB) is taken along the x-axis (horizontal line).
01041| To determine the output characteristics, the input current or emitter current IE is kept constant at
01042| zero mA and the output voltage VCB is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For
01043| each voltage level of the output voltage VCB, the output current (IC) is recorded.
01044| A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCB at constant input current
01045| IE (0 mA).
01046| When the emitter current or input current IE is equal to 0 mA, the transistor operates in the cut-off
01047| region.
01048| 
01049| Next, the input current (IE) is increased from 0 mA to 1 mA by adjusting the input voltage VBE and
01050| the input current IE is kept constant at 1 mA. While increasing the input current IE, the output
01051| voltage VCB is kept constant.
01052| After we kept the input current (IE) constant at 1 mA, the output voltage (VCB) is increased from
01053| zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of the output voltage (VCB), the output
01054| current (IC) is recorded.
01055| 
01056|  A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCB at constant input current
01057| IE (1 mA). This region is known as the active region of a transistor.
01058| This is repeated for higher fixed values of input current IE (I.e. 2 mA, 3 mA, 4 mA and so on).
01059| From the above characteristics, we can see that for a constant input current IE, when the output
01060| voltage VCB is increased, the output current IC remains constant.
01061| At saturation region, both emitter-base junction JE and collector-base junction JC are forward
01062| biased. From the above graph, we can see that a sudden increase in the collector current when the
01063| output voltage VCB makes the collector-base junction JC forward biased.
01064| Early effect
01065| Due to forward bias, the base-emitter junction JE acts as a forward biased diode and due to reverse
01066| bias, the collector-base junction JC acts as a reverse biased diode.
01067| Therefore, the width of the depletion region at the base-emitter junction JE is very small whereas
01068| the width of the depletion region at the collector-base junction JC is very large.
01069| If the output voltage VCB applied to the collector-base junction JC is further increased, the depletion
01070| region width further increases. The base region is lightly doped as compared to the collector region.
01071| So the depletion region penetrates more into the base region and less into the collector region. As
01072| a result, the width of the base region decreases. This dependency of base width on the output
01073| voltage (VCB) is known as an early effect.
01074| If the output voltage VCB applied to the collector-base junction JC is highly increased, the base
01075| width may be reduced to zero and causes a voltage breakdown in the transistor. This phenomenon
01076| is known as punch through.
01077| 
01078| Common Emitter Configuration
01079| In common emitter configuration, base is the input terminal, collector is the output terminal and
01080| emitter is the common terminal for both input and output. That means the base terminal and
01081| common emitter terminal are known as input terminals whereas collector terminal and common
01082| emitter terminal are known as output terminals.
01083| In common emitter configuration, the emitter terminal is grounded so the common emitter
01084| configuration is also known as grounded emitter configuration. Sometimes common emitter
01085| configuration is also referred to as CE configuration, common emitter amplifier, or CE amplifier.
01086| The common emitter (CE) configuration is the most widely used transistor configuration.
01087| 
01088|  The common emitter (CE) amplifiers are used when large current gain is needed.
01089| The input signal is applied between the base and emitter terminals while the output signal is taken
01090| between the collector and emitter terminals. Thus, the emitter terminal of a transistor is common
01091| for both input and output and hence it is named as common emitter configuration.
01092| The supply voltage between base and emitter is denoted by VBE while the supply voltage between
01093| collector and emitter is denoted by VCE.
01094| In common emitter (CE) configuration, input current or base current is denoted by IB and output
01095| current or collector current is denoted by IC.
01096| The common emitter amplifier has medium input and output impedance levels. So the current gain
01097| and voltage gain of the common emitter amplifier is medium. However, the power gain is high.
01098| To fully describe the behavior of a transistor with CE configuration, we need two set of
01099| characteristics вҖ“ input characteristics and output characteristics.
01100| Input characteristics
01101| The input characteristics describe the relationship between input current or base current (IB) and
01102| input voltage or base-emitter voltage (VBE).
01103| 
01104|  First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
01105| line represents x-axis. The input current or base current (IB) is taken along y-axis (vertical line)
01106| and the input voltage (VBE) is taken along x-axis (horizontal line).
01107| To determine the input characteristics, the output voltage VCE is kept constant at zero volts and the
01108| input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of
01109| input voltage (VBE), the corresponding input current (IB) is recorded.
01110| 
01111| A curve is then drawn between input current IB and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
01112| VCE (0 volts).
01113| Next, the output voltage (VCE) is increased from zero volts to certain voltage level (10 volts) and
01114| the output voltage (VCE) is kept constant at 10 volts. While increasing the output voltage (VCE),
01115| the input voltage (VBE) is kept constant at zero volts. After we kept the output voltage (VCE)
01116| constant at 10 volts, the input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels.
01117| For each voltage level of input voltage (VBE), the corresponding input current (IB) is recorded.
01118| A curve is then drawn between input current IB and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
01119| VCE (10 volts).
01120| This process is repeated for higher fixed values of output voltage (VCE).
01121| When output voltage (VCE) is at zero volts and emitter-base junction is forward biased by input
01122| voltage (VBE), the emitter-base junction acts like a normal p-n junction diode. So the input
01123| characteristics of the CE configuration is same as the characteristics of a normal pn junction diode.
01124| The cut in voltage of a silicon transistor is 0.7 volts and germanium transistor is 0.3 volts. In our
01125| case, it is a silicon transistor. So from the above graph, we can see that after 0.7 volts, a small
01126| increase in input voltage (VBE) will rapidly increases the input current (IB).
01127| 
01128|  In common emitter (CE) configuration, the input current (IB) is very small as compared to the input
01129| current (IE) in common base (CB) configuration. The input current in CE configuration is
01130| measured in microamperes (ОјA) whereas the input current in CB configuration is measured in
01131| milliamperes (mA).
01132| In common emitter (CE) configuration, the input current (IB) is produced in the base region which
01133| is lightly doped and has small width. So the base region produces only a small input current (IB).
01134| On the other hand, in common base (CB) configuration, the input current (IE) is produced in the
01135| emitter region which is heavily doped and has large width. So the emitter region produces a large
01136| input current (IE). Therefore, the input current (IB) produced in the common emitter (CE)
01137| configuration is small as compared to the common base (CB) configuration.
01138| Due to forward bias, the emitter-base junction acts as a forward biased diode and due to reverse
01139| bias, the collector-base junction acts as a reverse biased diode.
01140| Therefore, the width of the depletion region at the emitter-base junction is very small whereas the
01141| width of the depletion region at the collector-base junction is very large.
01142| If the output voltage VCE applied to the collector-base junction is further increased, the depletion
01143| region width further increases. The base region is lightly doped as compared to the collector region.
01144| So the depletion region penetrates more into the base region and less into the collector region. As
01145| a result, the width of the base region decreases which in turn reduces the input current (IB) produced
01146| in the base region.
01147| From the above characteristics, we can see that for higher fixed values of output voltage V CE, the
01148| curve shifts to the right side. This is because for higher fixed values of output voltage, the cut in
01149| voltage is increased above 0.7 volts. Therefore, to overcome this cut in voltage, more input voltage
01150| VBE is needed than previous case.
01151| Output characteristics
01152| The output characteristics describe the relationship between output current (IC) and output voltage
01153| (VCE).
01154| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
01155| line represents x-axis. The output current or collector current (IC) is taken along y-axis (vertical
01156| line) and the output voltage (VCE) is taken along x-axis (horizontal line).
01157| To determine the output characteristics, the input current or base current IB is kept constant at
01158| 0 ОјA and the output voltage VCE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each
01159| level of output voltage, the corresponding output current (IC) is recorded.
01160| 
01161|  A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCE at constant input current
01162| IB (0 ОјA).
01163| When the base current or input current IB = 0 ОјA, the transistor operates in the cut-off region. In
01164| this region, both junctions are reverse biased.
01165| Next, the input current (IB) is increased from 0 ОјA to 20 ОјA by adjusting the input voltage (VBE).
01166| The input current (IB) is kept constant at 20 ОјA.
01167| While increasing the input current (IB), the output voltage (VCE) is kept constant at 0 volts.
01168| After we kept the input current (IB) constant at 20 ОјA, the output voltage (VCE) is increased from
01169| zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of output voltage (VCE), the
01170| corresponding output current (IC) is recorded.
01171| A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCE at constant input current
01172| IB (20 ОјA). This region is known as the active region of a transistor. In this region, emitter-base
01173| junction is forward biased and the collector-base junction is reverse biased.
01174| This steps are repeated for higher fixed values of input current IB (I.e. 40 ОјA, 60 ОјA, 80 ОјA and
01175| so on).
01176| When output voltage VCE is reduced to a small value (0.2 V), the collector-base junction becomes
01177| forward biased. This is because the output voltage VCE has less effect on collector-base junction
01178| than input voltage VBE.
01179| 
01180|  As we know that the emitter-base junction is already forward biased. Therefore, when both the
01181| junctions are forward biased, the transistor operates in the saturation region. In this region, a small
01182| increase in output voltage VCE will rapidly increases the output current IC.
01183| 
01184| Common Collector Configuration
01185| In this configuration, the base terminal of the transistor serves as the input, the emitter terminal is
01186| the output and the collector terminal is common for both input and output. Hence, it is named as
01187| common collector configuration. The input is applied between the base and collector while the
01188| output is taken from the emitter and collector.
01189| In common collector configuration, the collector terminal is grounded so the common collector
01190| configuration is also known as grounded collector configuration.
01191| 
01192|  Sometimes common collector configuration is also referred to as emitter follower, voltage
01193| follower, common collector amplifier, CC amplifier, or CC configuration. This configuration is
01194| mostly used as a voltage buffer.

(Output truncated at 51200 bytes. Use 'offset' parameter to read beyond line 1194)
</file>
